question_text: Income and Expenditure Account of an NPO is equivalent to:
explanation: The Income and Expenditure Account of an NPO is equivalent to the Profit and Loss Account of a business entity. Both are prepared on an accrual basis and show revenue income and revenue expenditure to determine surplus/deficit (or profit/loss).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Cash Book equivalent is the Receipts & Payments Account, not I&E.
Balance Sheet shows financial position, not income vs expenditure.
Trading Account shows cost of goods â€” NPOs don't trade.

question_text: Income and Expenditure Account is prepared on:
explanation: The I&E Account is prepared on an ACCRUAL basis â€” it records income earned and expenses incurred during the year, whether or not cash has been received or paid. Adjustments for outstanding, prepaid, and accrued items are made.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Cash basis is used for R&P Account. I&E uses accrual.
There is no hybrid basis for I&E. It is purely accrual.
Receipt basis is another term for cash basis â€” used in R&P, not I&E.

question_text: Which of the following is shown in the Income & Expenditure Account?
explanation: Only REVENUE items (income and expenses) of the CURRENT YEAR are shown in the I&E Account. Subscriptions for the current year (revenue income) appear here. Capital items like building purchase or life membership fees do NOT appear in I&E.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Purchase of building is capital expenditure â€” shown on Balance Sheet, not I&E.
Life Membership Fees are capital receipts â€” added to Capital Fund on B/S.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating capital receipt as revenue income.

elimination_hints.hint: Legacy is a capital receipt â€” credited to Capital Fund, not I&E.

question_text: Subscriptions received during the year â‚ą5,00,000. Subscriptions outstanding at the end â‚ą40,000. Subscriptions received in advance â‚ą25,000. Subscriptions outstanding at the beginning â‚ą30,000. Subscriptions received in advance at the beginning â‚ą15,000. Subscription income for the I&E Account is:
explanation: Subscription for I&E = Received â€“ Outstanding at beginning â€“ Advance for next year + Outstanding at end + Advance at beginning = â‚ą5,00,000 â€“ â‚ą30,000 â€“ â‚ą25,000 + â‚ą40,000 + â‚ą15,000 = â‚ą5,00,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: You may have double-deducted. Check: subtract opening O/S and closing advance, add closing O/S and opening advance.
Check each adjustment carefully. Opening O/S is deducted (already received), not added.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding instead of deducting opening outstanding.

elimination_hints.hint: Verify: â‚ą5L â€“ â‚ą30K â€“ â‚ą25K + â‚ą40K + â‚ą15K = â‚ą5L.

question_text: Statement: Income and Expenditure Account records only revenue items of the current period.
explanation: This is TRUE. The I&E Account records only revenue income and revenue expenditure relating to the CURRENT accounting period. Capital items (like purchase of fixed assets, legacy, life membership fees) are excluded. Prior/next year items are adjusted out.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Capital items go to B/S. Prior/next year items are adjusted. Only current-year revenue items remain.

question_text: Entrance fees of a small and recurring nature should be treated as:
explanation: As per NCERT, entrance fees of small and recurring amounts are treated as revenue income and shown in the Income & Expenditure Account. If the amount is large and non-recurring, it is capitalised (added to Capital Fund).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Capital receipt added to Capital Fund

elimination_hints.hint: Capitalisation is done only when entrance fees are large and non-recurring.
Deferring is not the NCERT treatment â€” it is either revenue or capital.
Entrance fees are income for the NPO, not a liability.

question_text: General donations (non-specific, small amounts) are treated as:
explanation: General donations of small and non-specific nature are treated as REVENUE income and shown on the income (credit) side of the I&E Account. Only specific/large donations are capitalised.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Capital receipt added to Capital Fund
Shown as liability on Balance Sheet

elimination_hints.hint: Capitalisation applies to specific/large donations, not general small ones.
General donations are income for the NPO, not a liability.
Donations ADD to funds, they are never deducted from Capital Fund.

question_text: Salary paid during the year â‚ą3,60,000. Salary outstanding at the beginning â‚ą30,000. Salary outstanding at the end â‚ą45,000. Salary prepaid at the end â‚ą10,000. Salary expenditure for I&E Account is:
explanation: Salary for I&E = Paid â€“ Opening Outstanding + Closing Outstanding â€“ Closing Prepaid = â‚ą3,60,000 â€“ â‚ą30,000 + â‚ą45,000 â€“ â‚ą10,000 = â‚ą3,65,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: â‚ą3,60,000 is the cash paid â€” you need to adjust for outstanding and prepaid.
â‚ą3,75,000 = â‚ą3,60,000 â€“ â‚ą30K + â‚ą45K. Did you forget the prepaid deduction?
Check direction: opening outstanding is deducted (was paid this year for last year).

question_text: The balancing figure of the Income and Expenditure Account shows:
explanation: The I&E Account's balancing figure shows the Surplus (if income > expenditure) or Deficit (if expenditure > income) for the year. This is added to or deducted from the Capital Fund on the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Surplus or Deficit for the year

elimination_hints.hint: Cash balance is shown in the R&P Account, not I&E.
Capital Fund is determined from the Balance Sheet (Assets â€“ Liabilities).
Gross Profit is a trading concept â€” NPOs show Surplus, not GP.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Depreciation on fixed assets is shown in the Income & Expenditure Account, not in the Receipts & Payments Account.
Reason (R): Depreciation is a non-cash expense and R&P records only actual cash transactions.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Since R&P is cash-based, only actual cash flows are recorded. Depreciation involves no cash outflow â€” it is an allocation of cost. Therefore, it appears only in the I&E Account as an expense.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY depreciation is excluded from R&P â€” it's non-cash.
R is factually true â€” R&P does record only actual cash transactions.
A is correct â€” depreciation IS shown in I&E, not R&P.

question_text: Sale of old newspapers and magazines by a library is treated as:
explanation: Sale of old newspapers/magazines is a revenue income for the NPO. It is not a capital item (not a fixed asset disposal) â€” it is the sale of consumables/used items. It is shown on the income side of the I&E Account.
subtopic: Treatment of sale of old items
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Old newspapers are not fixed assets. Their sale is revenue, not capital.
Newspapers are not stationery. This is a separate income item.
Sale proceeds are income, not a deduction from Capital Fund.

question_text: A club received â‚ą50,000 for Tournament Fund. It spent â‚ą35,000 on the tournament. How should this be treated?
explanation: Tournament Fund is a specific fund. The fund receipt (â‚ą50,000) goes to the Balance Sheet as a liability/fund. Expenses (â‚ą35,000) are debited to this fund. The remaining â‚ą15,000 is shown as Tournament Fund on the Balance Sheet. Neither income nor expense goes to I&E.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Show â‚ą50,000 as income and â‚ą35,000 as expense in I&E Account
Show net â‚ą15,000 as income in I&E Account
Create Tournament Fund on B/S, debit expenses to the fund, show â‚ą15,000 balance on B/S
Show â‚ą35,000 as expense in I&E and â‚ą50,000 in Capital Fund

elimination_hints.hint: Specific fund items bypass the I&E Account entirely.
The net amount does not go to I&E â€” specific funds are handled on B/S.
Tournament fund is separate from Capital Fund. Don't mix the two.
elimination_hints.misconception: Merging specific fund with Capital Fund.

question_text: The Income & Expenditure Account has no ______ balance; it starts fresh each year.
explanation: The I&E Account has no OPENING balance. Unlike R&P which starts with opening cash/bank, the I&E Account is a nominal account that starts fresh every year. The surplus/deficit is transferred to Capital Fund and the account starts afresh.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: I&E does have a closing figure â€” the surplus or deficit.
I&E can have both credit (income) and debit (expenditure) entries.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking I&E has no credit entries.

elimination_hints.hint: I&E has debit entries for expenditure. The missing item is opening balance.

question_text: Subscriptions received â‚ą8,00,000 (including â‚ą60,000 for previous year and â‚ą40,000 for next year). Subscriptions outstanding at end â‚ą50,000. Subscription income for I&E Account is:
explanation: Subscriptions for current year = Total received â€“ Previous year â€“ Next year + Outstanding at end = â‚ą8,00,000 â€“ â‚ą60,000 â€“ â‚ą40,000 + â‚ą50,000 = â‚ą7,50,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: â‚ą8L includes previous and next year amounts â€” they must be excluded from current-year income.
â‚ą7L = â‚ą8L â€“ â‚ą60K â€“ â‚ą40K. You forgot to add outstanding at end (â‚ą50K).
You added instead of deducting previous/next year subscriptions.

question_text: Interest on investments of a specific fund (e.g., Prize Fund) should be:
explanation: Interest earned on investments of a specific fund belongs to that specific fund, not to the general I&E Account. It is added to the respective fund (e.g., Prize Fund) on the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Shown as income in I&E Account
Added to the respective specific fund on Balance Sheet
Ignored as it is not a cash receipt

elimination_hints.hint: Interest on a specific fund's investments belongs to that fund, not general income.
elimination_hints.misconception: Routing specific fund interest to I&E.

elimination_hints.hint: It is added to the specific fund, not deducted from Capital Fund.
Interest earned is recorded â€” it is not ignored. It goes to the specific fund.

question_text: Statement: The Income & Expenditure Account shows items of capital nature.
explanation: This is FALSE. The I&E Account shows ONLY revenue items (revenue income and revenue expenditure). Capital items like purchase of fixed assets, sale of investments, legacy, life membership fees, and specific donations are shown on the Balance Sheet, not in I&E.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: I&E only shows revenue items. Capital items go to the Balance Sheet.

question_text: If a club has 500 members each paying â‚ą1,200 annual subscription, and at year-end 40 members have not paid, the subscription income for I&E is:
explanation: Total subscription due = 500 Ă— â‚ą1,200 = â‚ą6,00,000. Since I&E is on accrual basis, ALL 500 members' subscriptions are included as income. The 40 unpaid (â‚ą48,000) appear as outstanding on the Balance Sheet. I&E income = â‚ą6,00,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: â‚ą5,52,000 = 460 Ă— â‚ą1,200 â€” this is cash received. I&E uses accrual, so include all 500.
â‚ą48,000 = 40 Ă— â‚ą1,200 is the outstanding amount, not total subscription.
Check: 500 Ă— â‚ą1,200 = â‚ą6,00,000. On accrual basis, full amount is income.

question_text: Which side of the I&E Account shows 'Expenditure'?
explanation: The DEBIT (left) side of the I&E Account shows all revenue expenditures (salary, rent, printing, depreciation, etc.). The CREDIT (right) side shows all revenue incomes (subscriptions, donations, interest, etc.). This is similar to the P&L Account format.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Neither side â€” it is shown separately

elimination_hints.hint: Credit side shows Income, not Expenditure.
Expenditure is only on debit side; Income on credit side.
Expenditure is integral to the I&E Account format.

question_text: Locker rent received â‚ą60,000 during the year. Locker rent receivable at beginning â‚ą8,000. Locker rent receivable at end â‚ą12,000. Locker rent income for I&E is:
explanation: Locker rent for I&E = Received â€“ Opening receivable + Closing receivable = â‚ą60,000 â€“ â‚ą8,000 + â‚ą12,000 = â‚ą64,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: â‚ą60,000 is the cash received. Need to adjust for outstanding amounts.
â‚ą56K = â‚ą60K â€“ â‚ą12K + â‚ą8K â€” you reversed the adjustments.
â‚ą80K = â‚ą60K + â‚ą8K + â‚ą12K. Opening receivable should be deducted.

question_text:
Match the following items with their treatment:

Column I:
(i) Annual subscription
(ii) Life Membership Fees
(iii) Sale of old sports material
(iv) Depreciation on furniture

Column II:
(P) Revenue income in I&E Account
(Q) Non-cash expense in I&E Account
(R) Capital receipt â€” Capital Fund on B/S
(S) Revenue income in I&E Account
explanation: Annual subscription â†’ Revenue income in I&E (P). Life Membership â†’ Capital receipt, Capital Fund on B/S (R). Sale of old sports material â†’ Revenue income in I&E (S). Depreciation â†’ Non-cash expense in I&E (Q).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Annual subscription is revenue income (P), not capital (R). Life membership is capital (R).
Life membership is capital (R), not non-cash expense (Q). Depreciation is non-cash (Q).
Both P and S say 'Revenue income in I&E'. (i) maps to P and (iii) maps to S â€” both are correct revenue items but with distinct keys.
